sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Need to position both greens at frames with correct colors, then click center to submit. Let me move right green to right frame.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 136 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking the right green at (31, 16) didn't change the centers. Both greens still have centers c and W. This is strange - clicking a green should swap centers, but it didn't.
Wait, maybe I clicked the wrong position. The right green is at cols 15-17, so the center is at col 16. Row 31 should be correct. But the centers didn't change.
Actually, looking more carefully, the centers stayed the same: left: c, right: W. So clicking the right green didn't swap centers. Maybe clicking only swaps centers when the green is at a specific position (like the bridge or c-square)?
Or maybe clicking a green only selects it (for movement), and the center swap happens only when clicking the center (row 31, col 32)?
Let me reconsider the mechanics:
- Clicking a green: selects it for movement
- Clicking center: swaps both greens' centers
- LEFT/RIGHT: moves the selected green (or all greens?)
But earlier, clicking the left green at (31, 10) did swap centers (left: c → W, right: W → c). And clicking the right green at (31, 28) also swapped centers. So clicking greens does swap centers.
But now, clicking the right green at (31, 16) didn't swap centers. Why?
Maybe the right green is not at (31, 16)? Let me check. The right green's boundary is [[30, 15], [30, 17], [32, 17], [32, 15]]. So the green spans rows 30-32 and cols 15-17. The center cell is at row 31, col 16. So I clicked the correct position.
Wait, maybe the click didn't register because the green is overlapping with the c-square? The c-square is at cols 11-15 (wait, no, the c-square in the left frame is at cols 11-15, and the right green is at cols 15-17. So the right green's left edge (col 15) overlaps with the c-square's right edge (col 15).
Actually, looking at the segmentation, there's only one c-square reported: boundary=[[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 44]]. This is the right c-square. The left c-square is not reported as a separate object. Maybe the left c-square is merged with the left frame?
Hmm, let me look at the ascii to see what's happening in the left frame.
Actually, I notice that the c-square child has color 'w' (light gray). The right green's center is 'W' (white). These are different colors. So the c-square interior is light gray, not white.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to match the green centers to the c-square interiors. The c-square interiors are 'w' (light gray). The green centers are 'c' (charcoal) or 'W' (white). Neither matches 'w'.
Wait, maybe I'm misinterpreting the colors. Let me check:
- 'w' = light gray
- 'W' = white
- 'c' = charcoal
These are all different colors. So the green centers don't match the c-square interiors.
Let me try yet another hypothesis: the goal is to have both greens with the same center color, positioned at specific locations. Currently, left: c, right: W (different). I need to make them the same.
To swap centers, I need to click a green. But clicking the right green at (31, 16) didn't work. Maybe I need to click the left green instead?
Let me try clicking the left green at (31, 10).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Clicking right green didn't swap centers! Maybe the green needs to be at a specific position for clicking to work. Let me try clicking the left green instead.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking left green to swap centers
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 10}])
print("After clicking left green:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  Green: boundary={n['boundary']}")
        for c in n['children']:
            cn = [x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == c][0]
            print(f"    Center: color={cn['color']}")

# Click center
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 32}])
print(f"\nAfter clicking center: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After clicking left green:
  Green: boundary=[[30, 9], [30, 11], [32, 11], [32, 9]]
    Center: color=W
  Green: boundary=[[30, 15], [30, 17], [32, 17], [32, 15]]
    Center: color=c

After clicking center: board_changed=True, level_completed=False
